headline: Burnett's 201 yards lift USC past Oklahoma in offensive shootout
body: USC escaped Norman with a 35-32 victory in a game that never felt like either offense would leave the field. Oklahoma's Arnold Glass authored one of those performances that should have won a shootout—388 yards, three touchdowns, and the Player of the Game honor—but USC's balanced attack and specifically Wes Burnett's eight-catch, 201-yard afternoon proved just enough to outlast the Sooners' air assault.

The difference in execution came down to yards after contact and staying out of their own way. USC's quarterback Marcus Moss completed fewer passes than Glass and threw the same number of touchdowns, yet the Trojans consistently moved the ball into the red zone and converted. Oklahoma's defense, anchored by Fiddler's seven tackles, never generated the pressure needed to disrupt Moss's timing with Burnett, who turned underneath routes into gain situations that wore down the Oklahoma secondary.

The fumble proved immaterial—both teams coughed it up once—but Oklahoma's inability to manufacture stops in the third and fourth quarters told the real story. USC's ground game was unremarkable on paper, but Myers's eight carries kept the chains moving and the Sooners' pass rush from pinning its ears back. When a team wins at home with a quarterback having that kind of statistical day and still falls short, the culprit usually isn't the arm talent; it's complementary football, and that belonged to USC on Saturday.
turningPoint: Burnett's 14-yard catch on third-and-11 late in the third quarter extended USC's go-ahead drive and effectively ended Oklahoma's momentum on a night when Glass needed any help the defense could provide.

unsungHero.name: Wes Burnett
unsungHero.why: Eight receptions for 201 yards won't generate Player of the Game votes when Glass threw for 388, but Burnett's consistency and the way he moved the chains against Oklahoma's secondary in critical moments was the real engine of USC's drive to victory.
